preamble: The phonetic shift from the voiceless labiodental fricative /f/ to the voiceless aspirated bilabial plosive /pʰ/ is an attested, though less common, sound change. This transformation primarily represents a form of fortition, where a fricative strengthens into a plosive. It often involves a change in the place of articulation from labiodental to bilabial, accompanied by the addition of aspiration. This shift is frequently observed in the adaptation of loanwords into languages that lack the /f/ sound in their native phonological inventory, or as a regular dialectal correspondence.
phoneticEffects: Fortition (fricative to plosive), change in place of articulation (labiodental to bilabial), addition of aspiration

examples.note: English loanwords with /f/ are adapted to /pʰ/ (e.g., "phone" becomes /pʰon/).
The voiceless labiodental fricative [f] in Chittagonian corresponds to the Bangla voiceless aspirated bilabial plosive [pʰ].
In colloquial speech, /f/ may be simplified to /pʰ/. Some Korean borrowings also convert /f/ to /pʰ/.

languageExamples.language: Indian English (Oriya, Bengali, Hindi speaking varieties)
